text: The surgeon uses a conical stem for the hip replacement, similar to the contralateral side, through a posterior approach. The surgical field shows the posterior aspect of the hip, with visible anatomical structures relevant to this approach.

The surgeon places a checkpoint on the greater trochanter and anchors a ray with three Stein pins into the iliac crest percutaneously. A stabilizer is positioned on the iliac crest for acetabular orientation. The baseline limb length and offset are checked, and after dislocating the hip, the lesser to center distance is measured using a robotic system.

The surgeon cuts the femoral neck and removes the femoral head, followed by soft tissue releases to optimize acetabular exposure. Due to scarring from a prior pelvic osteotomy, the redundant acetabular labrum is removed, and a checkpoint is placed on the pelvis.

The surgeon registers the checkpoint and three divots on the stabilizer for the acetabulum. Points are taken inside the acetabular concavity and around its periphery to register the acetabular bone. Registration is confirmed by popping bubbles, indicating an accurate match to the native anatomy. The setup is now ready for the introduction of the robotic system.

The surgeon uses a robotic reamer, initially two millimeters smaller than the final target size, to ream the acetabulum. Due to dysplasia, the reamer may skip if the acetabular concavity is insufficient. After establishing the desired center of rotation, the plan is adjusted to ream two millimeters larger for a concentric ream. The multi-hole component is then impacted using the robotic arm, achieving excellent press fit with precise inclination and aversion.

The surgeon takes five points around the margin of the acetabular component to assess the achieved version, comparing it to the planned cut position relative to bony landmarks. Multiple screws are used to supplement fixation of the acetabular component. The focus then shifts to the femur.

The surgeon removes the trial implant and reams deeper into the femur before inserting the actual device several millimeters deeper, along with the planned head. The hip is reduced, checkpoints and arrays are removed, and the hip capsule is closed using a transosseous repair. The quadratus femoris is closed, followed by a layered closure from the fascia up to the skin.
